failure_surface: Repeated syntax errors preventing code execution, combined with incomplete handling of formatting preservation requirements
root_cause: Agent incorrectly formatted code blocks causing tool invocation tags to interfere with Python execution; additionally created new Workbook instead of modifying existing one, losing original formatting
minimal_fix_hypothesis: Ensure pure Python code is passed without wrapping markdown or tool syntax artifacts; when formatting preservation is required, load the existing workbook with openpyxl and modify in-place rather than creating a new Workbook object
evidence: Multiple attempts show 'SyntaxError: invalid syntax' on line 1, indicating code block corruption
First attempt included natural language text 'Let me create the complete solution file directly:' before tool tag
All attempts used 'new_wb = Workbook()' then saved new file instead of modifying loaded 'wb' workbook
Task requested unmerging cells, font changes, and date formatting preservation - these require in-place modification
Input file was .xlsm (macro-enabled) but agent treated it as .xlsx format

memory_items.title: Code Block Hygiene for Execution
memory_items.lesson: When executing Python code through tools, ensure only valid Python syntax is included - no tool tags, markdown artifacts, or explanatory text should be embedded in the executable code stream.
memory_items.generalization: Always validate that code sent to execution environments contains only syntactically valid statements; remove any wrapper markers or natural language before execution.

memory_items.title: Preserve Formatting via In-Place Modification
memory_items.lesson: When spreadsheet tasks require maintaining formatting (fonts, colors, borders, merged cells), load the existing workbook with openpyxl and apply modifications directly rather than creating a new Workbook and writing data fresh.
memory_items.generalization: Use openpyxl's cell object manipulation on the original Workbook to retain styling information; never convert to pandas DataFrame and back if formatting must be preserved.

memory_items.title: Match Tool Choice to Task Requirements
memory_items.lesson: Verify whether the task requires formula retention, macro functionality, or specific file formats before selecting between pandas (data processing) and openpyxl (formatting/preservation).
memory_items.generalization: For tasks involving merged cells, styles, formulas, or macro-enabled workbooks (.xlsm), use openpyxl exclusively; avoid pandas.to_excel() for formatting-sensitive operations.

edits.content:
### Trace-Derived Lessons

**Code Block Hygiene**: When executing Python code through tools, ensure only valid Python syntax is included - no tool tags, markdown artifacts, or explanatory text should be embedded in the executable code stream. Always validate that code sent to execution environments contains only syntactically valid statements.

**Preserve Formatting via In-Place Modification**: When spreadsheet tasks require maintaining formatting (fonts, colors, borders, merged cells), load the existing workbook with openpyxl and apply modifications directly rather than creating a new Workbook and writing data fresh. Use openpyxl's cell object manipulation on the original Workbook to retain styling information.

**Match Tool Choice to Task Requirements**: Verify whether the task requires formula retention, macro functionality, or specific file formats before selecting between pandas (data processing) and openpyxl (formatting/preservation). For tasks involving merged cells, styles, formulas, or macro-enabled workbooks, use openpyxl exclusively; avoid pandas.to_excel() for formatting-sensitive operations.
